coredata.dc:description: Biomass-based solar evaporators offer a sustainable approach to freshwater production; however, their efficiency is often limited. In this study, we developed a method by using only sulfuric acid to convert waste sawdust into an efficient photothermal conversion material, which contains plenty of carbon dots (CDs)/carbon nanospheres (CNSs) on the surface of rod-like microparticles, similar to the appearance of bitter melons. We refer to these microparticles as bitter melon-like microparticles (BMPs). When integrated with bacterial cellulose (BC), these BMPs form a film (BMPs-BC) that exhibits superior light absorption (94.6 %) and efficient solar water evaporation, achieving a water evaporation rate of up to 3.53 kg m−2 h−1 under one sun irradiation. The synergistic interaction between BC and BMPs creates ultra-thin water layers and large amounts of intermediate water, significantly reducing the enthalpy of water evaporation from 2409.6 J g−1 to 1003.4 J g−1. Long-term outdoor experiments demonstrated a maximum evaporation rate of 2.86 kg m−2 h−1 and a daily freshwater production of up to 15.9 kg m−2 on the BMPs-BC evaporator arrays. This research provides a promising strategy for utilizing abundant waste sawdust to create sustainable, low-cost, and highly efficient solar evaporators, thereby contributing to the alleviation of global water scarcity.

head.ce:title: One-step conversion of waste sawdust into bitter melon-like particles for efficient solar-driven water evaporation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biomass-based solar evaporators offer a sustainable approach to freshwater production; however, their efficiency is often limited. In this study, we developed a method by using only sulfuric acid to convert waste sawdust into an efficient photothermal conversion material, which contains plenty of carbon dots (CDs)/carbon nanospheres (CNSs) on the surface of rod-like microparticles, similar to the appearance of bitter melons. We refer to these microparticles as bitter melon-like microparticles (BMPs). When integrated with bacterial cellulose (BC), these BMPs form a film (BMPs-BC) that exhibits superior light absorption (94.6 %) and efficient solar water evaporation, achieving a water evaporation rate of up to 3.53 kg m−2 h−1 under one sun irradiation. The synergistic interaction between BC and BMPs creates ultra-thin water layers and large amounts of intermediate water, significantly reducing the enthalpy of water evaporation from 2409.6 J g−1 to 1003.4 J g−1. Long-term outdoor experiments demonstrated a maximum evaporation rate of 2.86 kg m−2 h−1 and a daily freshwater production of up to 15.9 kg m−2 on the BMPs-BC evaporator arrays. This research provides a promising strategy for utilizing abundant waste sawdust to create sustainable, low-cost, and highly efficient solar evaporators, thereby contributing to the alleviation of global water scarcity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The continuous growth of the global population, accelerated industrialization, and the drought problems caused by climate change have exacerbated the demand for freshwater resources [1–3]. Seawater, with its abundant reserves of both freshwater and valuable minerals [4], offers a critical source for addressing the escalating freshwater crisis. In such a context, seawater desalination technology becomes increasingly crucial. However, traditional methods of freshwater acquisition are unable to meet the growing demand, driving the urgent need for more sustainable and economical alternative solutions [5]. Solar-driven interfacial evaporation, as a sustainable solution, has garnered significant attention due to its potential in water resource treatment and energy sustainability [6–9]. Despite its enormous potential, the cost of materials remains a constraining factor. Most current solar-driven interfacial evaporation devices utilize expensive materials such as metal nanoparticles [10–13], inorganic semiconductor [14,15], graphene [16,17], carbon nanotubes [18,19], or specialized porous materials [20,21], thereby increasing production costs and limiting their application in developing countries and resource-poor regions. Therefore, finding a low-cost and efficient preparation method is crucial for advancing the practical application of solar-driven interfacial evaporation technology.

In the search for a simple, low-cost, and scalable preparation method for preparation of solar interfacial materials, biomass-derived carbon materials have attracted considerable attention. Compared to other solar interfacial materials, biomass-derived carbon materials offer many advantages of low cost, sustainable supply, extensive availability, and easy accessibility [22–25]. Thus, biomass-derived carbon materials hold tremendous potential in the field of solar interfacial materials, offering new solutions for efficient solar-driven water evaporation. Carbon dots (CDs) are a class of carbon-based nanomaterials with unique optical and electronic properties, with biomass being one of their common carbon sources. The synthesis methods of carbon dots can be broadly categorized into two approaches: “top-down” and “bottom-up.” The “top-down” synthesis method involves breaking macroscopic carbon materials into nanoscale carbon dots. The primary techniques include arc discharge [26], laser ablation [27], oxidation treatment [28], and ball milling [29]. These methods generally require advanced equipment and typically low yield. The “bottom-up” synthesis method utilizes organic small molecules or polymers as carbon sources, which are carbonized at high temperatures to produce carbon dots. Common methods include hydrothermal/solvothermal synthesis and microwave synthesis [30,31]. However, these methods often necessitate high-temperature and high-pressure conditions and involve complex purification steps. Compared to traditional methods that rely on high-temperature and high-pressure conditions, the concentrated sulfuric acid carbonization method offers significant advantages in simplicity and cost-effectiveness.

In modern society, wood is commonly used as the primary raw material for papermaking, construction, and furniture manufacturing. However, during large-scale industrial production, a large amount of sawdust is generated as a by-product. These waste materials from agricultural or industrial activities are often considered valueless and are frequently disposed of by open-air burning or used as fillers. This practice not only damages the ecological environment but also reduces resource utilization [32,33]. Therefore, utilizing the concentrated sulfuric acid carbonization method to convert waste sawdust into valuable biomass-derived carbon materials can not only solve waste disposal problems but also provide low-cost and efficient materials for solar-driven interfacial evaporation technology, thereby promoting its widespread application in practical use.

In this study, we demonstrated that waste sawdust after sulfuric acid treatment can be converted to bitter melon-like particles (BMPs) with CNSs and CDs on the surface, and the films made by such particles with bacterial cellulose can be applied as the interfacial material for efficient solar-driven water evaporation. Compared to direct pyrolysis, laser-induced and hydrothermal methods, sulfuric acid treatment does not require high-pressure and high-temperature conditions, making it easier to control the reaction process. Using sawdust as the raw material not only addresses the issue of waste disposal but also reduces material costs. CNSs and CDs contribute to high photothermal conversion efficiency (95 %) and reduce water evaporation enthalpy from 2409.6 J g−1 to 1003.4 J g−1, significantly improving the performance of the solar evaporator designed for freshwater generation. Indoor evaporation tests showed that the water evaporation rate of the BMPs-BC solar evaporator was 3.53 kg m−2 h−1. Furthermore, the maximum water generation rate of these solar evaporators in outdoor tests was 2.86 kg m−2 h−1, and they can achieve a daily drinkable freshwater production of up to 15.9 kg m−2.

Sawdust was purchased from Hebei Jiukuo New Materials Co., Ltd., China. Acetic acid (AR 99 %), sodium chlorite (NaClO2, 99 %), ethanol (AR 99 %), acetone (AR 99 %), lignin (AR 99 %), and cellulose (AR 99 %) were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Chemical Co., Ltd. Sulfuric acid (95 %–98 %) was purchased from Beijing Tongguang Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. Milli-Q water was used in all experiments.

First, the sawdust was ground into powder and sieved through a 50-mesh screen to obtain uniform particles. Then, 1 g of the sieved sawdust was dispersed in 100 mL of deionized water and treated with a high-pressure homogenizer at pressures of 100, 200, 400, 600, 800, and 1000 MPa for 10 min each to obtain homogenized sawdust dispersions. Subsequently, the dispersed sawdust was freeze-dried to obtain sawdust powder treated with the high-pressure homogenizer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The process of reducing the lignin content in sawdust
ce:section.ce:para: NaClO2 was dissolved into water to prepare a 5 % solution, followed by the addition of acetic acid to adjust the pH to 4.6. The sawdust after high-pressure homogenization was immersed in this NaClO2 solution at 80 °C for 8 h until the samples became completely white. The bleached sawdust was washed repeatedly with distilled water and dried in an oven at 40 °C.

The homogenized sawdust (5 g) was first extracted with acetone (300 mL) in a Soxhlet apparatus at 60 °C for 4 h to remove lipophilic components. Then, deionized water (150 mL) was added to the acetone extracted sawdust, and stirred vigorously at 25 °C for 3 h. The suspension was filtered through a Buchner funnel and washed with 50 mL of deionized water. The solid precipitate was sawdust with low hemicellulose content. The liquid filtrate was concentrated to approximately 10 mL by a rotary evaporator in a water bath set at 45 °C. The concentrated filtrate was then added to ethanol, with the volume percentage of ethanol being 90 %. Then, this mixture was allowed to precipitate overnight in a refrigerator at −10 °C. The solid precipitate was collected by centrifugation at 3000 rpm for 5 min, then washed twice with ethanol, and finally dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C to obtain hemicellulose [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The process of reducing the content of lignin and hemicellulose in sawdust
ce:section.ce:para: The sawdust with lower hemicellulose content was processed using the method described in Section 2.1.3 to obtain sawdust for lower hemicellulose and lignin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs, BMPs, CDs-SD and C-SD
ce:section.ce:para: Sulfuric acid (95 %–98 %) and deionized water were mixed in a 2:1 volume ratio to prepare the sulfuric acid solution. Under continuous stirring, 0.5 g of pretreated sawdust was added to 50 mL of the sulfuric acid solution and reacted at 80 °C for 12 h. After the reaction was complete, the reaction solution was diluted tenfold with deionized water to reduce acidity. Then, the mixture was filtered to collect the solid product, which was washed several times with deionized water until the filtrate was neutral, resulting in the final product as bitter melon-like microparticles (BMPs). BMPs was dispersed into the deionized water to obtain the dispersion of BMPs (20 mg/g). The filtrate was dialyzed (retention = 3.5 kDa) for 72 h, then freeze dry to obtain CDs powder. The CDs-SD and C-SD were also prepared in the same method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of C-CDs, H-CDs and L-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Sulfuric acid (95 %–98 %) and deionized water were mixed in a 2:1 volume ratio to prepare the sulfuric acid solution. Under continuous stirring, 0.5 g of cellulose nanocrystals was added to 50 mL of the sulfuric acid solution and reacted at 80 °C for 12 h. After the reaction was complete, the reaction solution was diluted tenfold with deionized water to reduce acidity. Then, solid sodium hydroxide was slowly added to neutralize the remaining sulfuric acid until the solution reached a neutral pH. Then filter the neutralized mixture to collect the filtrate. The filtrate as dialyzed (retention = 3.5 kDa) for 72 h, then freeze dry to obtain C-CDs powder. The L-CDs and H-CDs were also prepared in the same method.

To prepare the culture medium, 38.9 g of HS medium was mixed with 1 L of deionized water, and then the solution was placed in an autoclave (Zhiwei A, Xiamen, China) at 121 °C for 20 min. K. sucrofermentans was cultured with high-pressure and high-temperature sterilized HS basic medium at 30 °C for 9 days. Then, the obtained BC was treated with NaOH solution (2 %, w/v, 20 mL) at 60 °C for 12 h and then washed with deionized water. The BC film cleaned with deionized water was crushed to form a dispersion (10 mg/g) using a blender.

The solid (5.6) was washed multiple times with deionized water and dispersed in deionized water to obtain the dispersion of BMPs (20 mg/g). The BMPs dispersion (0.7 g) and the BC dispersion (0.6 g) were mixed, and then the mixed suspension was filtered to obtain BMPs-BC film. The CDs-SD-BC and NLH-SD-BC were also prepared in the same method.

The morphology of the sawdust, BMPs and BMPs-BC film were investigated by using scanning electron microscopy (SU8010, Hitachi Ltd., Japan). The samples were mounted onto an aluminum stub, coated by a layer of platinum with MC1000 (Hitachi Ltd., Japan). The CDs were observed by transmission electron microscopy (HT-7700Exalens, Hitachi Ltd., Japan). To prepare the specimens, the CDs were dispersed in deionized water with a concentration of 1 μg/mL, then 10 μL of the CDs suspension was dropped onto the surface of an ultra-thin carbon coated copper grid and dried completely. The fluorescence spectra of CDs were obtained by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (FS5, Edinburgh Ltd., UK). The absorption spectra of CDs, BMPs and BMPs-BC film were obtained by UV–Visible-NIR spectrophotometer (UV-3600 plus, Shimadzu Ltd., Japan). The crystallinity of CDs, BMPs and BMPs-BC film were measured by XRD (D8 Advance, Bruker, Germany). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained on a Nicolet 6 VERTEX 70 FTIR spectrometer (Bruker AXS., Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a K-Alpha electron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Ltd., USA). The surface temperature of the samples was obtained by the infrared thermal camera (E52, FLIR Ltd., USA). The Raman spectra of water in CDs, BMPs and BMPs-BC film were recorded in Via spectrometer (Renishaw Via) with an excitation wavelength of 532 nm. The latent heat of water evaporation analysis was conducted on a Differential Scanning Calorimeter instrument (DSC, 200F3, NETZSCH) at a test temperature of 45 °C, each sample was placed in an aluminum crucible with a small hole in the lid. The concentrations of Na+, K+, Ca2+, and Mg2+ in the collected water were analyzed using an inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (ICP-OES, Agilent 5110). The water contact angle tests were conducted using the sessile drop method with a DSA-100S contact angle analyzer (KRÜSS, Germany).

To determine the water evaporation rate, a glass beaker (diameter ≈ 60 mm and 10 cm in height) was used to store water, and an 8 mm thick polyethylene foam board was used as the insulation layer. A piece of filter paper was placed on the upper surface of the polyethylene foam, ensuring direct contact with the water to facilitate water transport. Subsequently, a BMPs-BC film was positioned on the filter paper to enhance sunlight absorption. Indoor solar evaporator tests were performed using a solar simulator (SD-S500, China Education Au-light Ltd., China) with an outputting solar flux at 1 kW m−2 (equivalent to onesun irradiation). The light intensity was calibrated before each test by using a thermal power sensor (S401C, Thorlabs Ltd., USA) connected to a handheld digital power meter (PM100, Thorlabs Ltd., USA).

In the outdoor experiments, a larger glass container (10 cm in length, 6 cm in width, and 10 cm in height) was used to store the water, with an 8 mm thick polyethylene foam board serving as the insulation layer. A piece of filter paper was also placed on the upper surface of the polyethylene foam, ensuring direct contact with the water to facilitate water transport. Subsequently, twenty pieces of BMPs-BC films were arranged in a 5 × 4 array on the filter paper to enhance sunlight absorption. The solar irradiation, temperature, and humidity during the outdoor tests were recorded by a portable weather station (YG-BX, Wuhan Chenyun Technology Co., Ltd., China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of solar energy-to-vapor generation efficiency
ce:section.ce:para:
The solar conversion efficiency ( η ) is the ratio of the thermal energy stored in the vapors to the total solar flux input, expressed by the following equation:

g is the evaporation rate per unit area of the BMPs-BC film within the same environmental condition (When calculating the equivalent enthalpy of evaporation, the evaporation area we used includes both of the projected area and the four sides of the BMPs-BC film.

In order to prove that the evaporation enthalpy of water in BMPs-BC films was reduced, Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC, 200F3, NETZSCH) measurement is used to measure the vaporization energy of pure water and water in BMPs-BC films. During the evaporation enthalpy measurements, the BMPs-BC film was placed in an Al crucible with a hole and measured with linear heating rate 5 °C min−1, under nitrogen flow flux (50 mL min−1), in the temperature range from 10 to 200 °C. Fig. S5c shows the change of heat flow signal as a function of temperature. For pure water, a sharp peak can be observed and the heat flow signal decreased dramatically after the signal reaches maximum. This indicates the water evaporation is completed immediately. However, for all the BMPs-BC films, the peaks are broader than that of the pure water, and the heat flow signal showed a gradual decay instead, indicating that the water evaporation in BMPs-BC films is different from pure water. The measured evaporation enthalpy of water is 2451 J g−1, which is very close to the theoretical value of 2444 J g−1, suggesting the good measurement accuracy. The measured enthalpy of evaporation of BMPs-BC film is 1762.3 J g−1, which is higher than the relative enthalpy of evaporation (1043 J g−1). Because the DSC test and relative enthalpy of evaporation test present a full dehydration and slightly dehydration processes, respectively [37,38].

To avoid the above issues, we have made improvements to the traditional testing method. Firstly, place the BMPs-BC film in an Al crucible with holes and weigh to obtain m
1 . Afterwards, place the Al crucible containing BMPs-BC film and maintain it at 45 °C (the operating temperature of the evaporator) for 20 min under nitrogen flow rate (50 mL min−1). The Al crucible containing BMPs-BC was re-weighed as m
2 . Obtain the energy Q absorbed by BMPs-BC film during the testing period by integrating the DSC curve.

As shown in Fig. 6d, the evaporation enthalpy of pure water was approximately 2409.6 J g−1, close to the standard value of 2444 J g−1, while the evaporation enthalpy of water in BMPs-BC film was 1003.4 J g−1 (Fig. 5d), which is close to the equivalent vaporization enthalpy (1043 J g−1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Freezing-melting behavior of water by DSC measurement
ce:section.ce:para: Freezing and melting behavior of water were tests using differential scanning calorimetry (DSC, 200F3, NETZSCH). The sample was closely sealed in an Al crucible to prevent the evaporation of water and kept at −20 °C for 20 min to be fully frozen. The data was scanned at a linear heating rate of 1 °C min−1 over a temperature range of −20 °C to 20 °C.

This study developed a simple method to prepare bitter melon-like particles from sawdust through sulfuric acid treatment, then these particles were integrated with BC to from films (BMPs-BC) for interfacial material used in solar-driven water evaporator (Fig. 1 ). The length and width of the original sawdust particles exhibited a size of approximately were 50–250 μm and 14–57 μm respectively (Fig. 2a), with a relatively smooth surface (Fig. S1a), which was attributed to the parallel alignment of fiber structures forming a dense matrix through hydrogen bonding, while being enveloped by pectin and waxy substances. Following high-pressure homogenization, the length and width of the particle size were significantly reduced to approximately 8–36 μm and 0.12–1.34 μm respectively (Fig. S2b), accompanied by the generation of a substantial amount of smaller debris, the length measuring less than 1 μm (Fig. 2b). This disruption was attributed to the diffusion of high-pressure steam into the interior of the sawdust fibers and the occurrence of an explosion, leading to structural breakdown and reducing the density of interconnections between lignin, hemicellulose, and cellulose [39,40]. X-ray Diffraction (XRD) analysis indicated that high-pressure homogenization did not alter the crystalline structure type of the sawdust (Fig. S2). However, a decrease in crystallinity from 55.9 % to 50.3 % was observed compared to the pristine sawdust (Eq. (1)). This reduction in crystallinity may have been caused by the disruption of cellulose crystals induced by steam [41].

After sulfuric acid treatment, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image shows that the obtained particles exhibit a structure similar to bitter melons, with a large number of nanospheres formed on the rod-like surface (Fig. 2c), with an average diameter of 46.02 ± 6.34 nm (Figs. 2d, S3). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image also shows the presence of both nanospheres and abundant CDs on the surface of the BMPs (Fig. 2e). These CDs were spherical nanoparticles with diameters ranging from 1.3 nm to 5.8 nm, with an average diameter of 3.5 nm (Figs. 2f, S4, S5). The BC pellicle shows a three-dimensional interconnected porous nanofiber network (Fig. 2g), with diameters ranging from 10 to 100 nm (Fig. S6). The surface and cross-sectional morphology of the films formed by BC and BMPs was shown in Fig. 2h and i, respectively. The BC was coated on the surface of BMPs and interconnected to form a free-standing film, while the BMPs were interconnected by the BC. These interconnected structures are conducive to the efficient transport of water molecules.

The fluorescence emission spectra show that the CDs exhibit fluorescence emission under various excitation wavelengths, with emission wavelengths gradually shifting towards the red as the excitation wavelength increases (Fig. 3a). Notably, fluorescence emission reaches its maximum intensity under an excitation at 380 nm, with the optimal emission peak at 482 nm. Secondly, the UV–visible absorption spectrum indicates that the CDs primarily absorb ultraviolet light, extending into the near-infrared region (Fig. S7). Characteristic absorption peak in the spectrum at 282 nm is typically attributed to π-π* transitions of CC bonds. Further Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis reveals the composition of functional groups on the surface of CDs (Fig. S8). Absorption peaks at 3677 cm−1, 2904 cm−1, 1247 cm−1, 1065 cm−1, 812 cm−1 were assigned to OH, CH, SO, CO, and SO vibration respectively, indicating the presence of hydroxyl, carboxyl, and sulfonic acid groups on the CD surface [42,43]. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) further shows the chemical composition of the CDs, primarily composed of C (74.77 %), N (1.64 %), O (22.84 %), and S (0.75 %) (Fig. S9). Additionally, high-resolution C spectra (Fig. S10) show four distinct types of C: CC (284.27 eV), CC (284.94 eV), CO (286.18 eV), and CO (288.13 eV). High-resolution O spectra (Fig. S11) show three types of O: CO (531.86 eV), CO (532.90 eV), and SO3H (530.60 eV). These XPS results further indicate the presence of hydroxyl, carboxyl, and sulfonic acid groups on the CD surface, which is consistent with the FTIR results.

The crystalline structure characteristics of CDs and BMPs were investigated by using XRD analysis and shown in Fig. 3b. The CDs exhibited a broad diffraction peak at 22.9°, a typical feature of amorphous carbon. The BMPs shows characteristic cellulose I diffraction peaks at 2θ = 15.3° (110) and 22.3° (200), indicating the preservation of the internal cellulose structure [44]. Notably, the peak at 22.3° for BMPs is enlarged compared to sawdust, primarily due to the presence of CDs on BMPs. Raman spectroscopy was used to investigate the graphitic structure of the CDs and BMPs. As shown in Fig. 3c (red line), for the CDs, the D band at 1327 cm−1 represents amorphous carbon, while the G band at 1572 cm−1 corresponds to graphitic carbon. The relative intensity ratio of the D band to the G band (ID/IG) is widely used to characterize the degree of graphitization in carbon materials [45]. The ID/IG value for the CDs is approximately 0.51. The BMPs also exhibits both D and G bands, indicating the successful generation of CDs from sawdust (Fig. 3c, black line). Additionally, CH stretch of cellulose located at 2940 cm−1 was observed in the Raman spectrum of BMPs (Fig. 3d), further confirming the preservation of the cellulose component from pristine sawdust to BMPs [46], which is consistent with XRD results (Fig. 3b). FTIR analysis reveals the presence of sulfonic acid groups in BMPs compared to the pristine sawdust (Fig. 3e) [42,43,47,48]. The full-scan XPS spectra show a decrease in oxygen content from 39.0 % to 30.7 % and an increase in carbon content from 58.2 % to 61.3 % in the BMPs compared to the pristine sawdust (Fig. 3f). Additionally, S (5.11 %) was presented in the BMPs. High-resolution C spectra show a decrease in CO and CC bonds in the BMPs compared to that of the pristine sawdust (Fig. 3g). High-resolution O and S spectra further indicate the presence of sulfonic acid groups (Fig. 3h and i), which is consistent with the results of the FTIR characterization (Fig. 3e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of water evaporation properties of BMPs-BC evaporator
ce:section.ce:para: We fabricated BMPs-BC film by mixing BMPs with BC nanofibers at certain ratios for the application as solar-driven interfacial evaporators. In this type of evaporators, the BMPs convert sunlight into thermal energy to heat seawater, while the BC nanofibers facilitate the transportation of water to the surface of the evaporators. Initially, we determined the optimal ratio of the BMPs to BC nanofibers. As shown in Fig. 4a, increasing the proportion of BMPs enhances the light absorption capacity of the BMPs-BC film. The light absorption rate was calculated using the method shown in Eq. (2). The light absorption results were summarized and presented in Fig. S12, with the BC alone showing a light absorption rate of 32.7 %, whereas the BMPs alone achieved 95.6 % of absorption. Then, we evaluated the evaporation performance of the BMPs-BC using a home-built system as shown in Fig. 4b. Under one sun irradiation, the evaporation rate of the BMPs-BC evaporator first increased and then decreased with an increasing proportion of the BMPs, reaching a maximum rate of 3.53 kg m−2 h−1 when the weight ratio of the BMPs to the BC nanofibers was 7:3 by weight (Fig. 4c). For comparison, the evaporation rate of pure water without the BMPs-BC was 0.26 kg m−2 h−1 (Fig. S13). The temperature changes of the BMPs-BC film during the tests were monitored using an infrared camera (Fig. 4d). The temperature of the BMPs-BC film rapidly increased from 16.4 °C to 45.0 °C within 5 min and then stabilized (Fig. S14).

Compared to pure water, salt deposition on the surfaces of conventional evaporators operating in seawater would usually significantly reduce the water evaporation rate. We conducted experiments using simulated seawater (3.5 % by weight NaCl solution) and saturated sodium chloride solution (26.5 % by weight NaCl) to assess the salt resistance of our BMPs-BC evaporators. As shown in Fig. 4e, the water evaporation rates of the BMPs-BC evaporator remained almost consistent in both pure water and simulated seawater (3.49 kg m−2 h−1), and show a small decrease in the saturated saline solution, but it was still as high as 3.30 kg m−2 h−1. The performance of the evaporators was also evaluated using real seawater from the Yellow Sea (Qingdao, China). As shown in Fig. 4f, after continuous testing for 24 h in real seawater, the evaporation rate remained stable as 3.46 kg m−2 h−1, and no salt accumulation was observed during the testing process. This indicates the excellent salt resistance of the BMPs-BC evaporator. The stability of the evaporation capacity of the BMPs-BC evaporator in real seawater was also evaluated through a cyclic testing, showing no significant decrease in water evaporation rate even after 10 cycles (Fig. 4g). The significant salt resistance of the BMPs-BC evaporator is mainly attributed to its hydrophilicity. This characteristic was confirmed through contact angle measurements, water droplets were rapidly absorbed once contacting with the surface of BMPs-BC film, demonstrating their high hydrophilicity (Fig. S15). When the evaporator was tested in saltwater, water was transported to the evaporation interface through the BC. Due to concentration gradients, high-concentration salt at the evaporation interface was transported back to the solution. Meanwhile, the hydrophilicity of the BMPs-BC also facilitates the diffusion of high-concentration salt at the evaporation interface, thereby hindering salt precipitation. The water evaporation rate under one sun irradiation is a key parameter for assessing the performance of the evaporator. Therefore, we compared the performance of the BMPs-BC evaporator with recently reported evaporators based on natural materials and summarized them in a figure. As we can see in Fig. 4h and Table S1, the evaporation rate of the BMPs-BC evaporators exceeded that of most evaporators made with biomass-derived carbon materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of evaporation mechanism of BMPs-BC evaporator
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the formation mechanism of BMPs, pure cellulose, lignin, and hemicellulose were individually treated with 65 % sulfuric acid under the same conditions for preparation of BMPs. The resulted particles were characterized by using SEM and shown in Fig. S16a, b, and c, respectively. Using lignin alone as the raw material in sulfuric acid treatment produces nanospheres with a diameter of approximately 41.5 nm (Fig. S16c1), which is consistent with the size of the nanospheres in BMPs. Therefore, it is possible that the lignin component in the sawdust contributes to generate these nanospheres on the surface of sawdust during sulfuric acid treatment. The CDs derived from cellulose, lignin, and hemicellulose were characterized using TEM and named C-CDs, H-CDs, and L-CDs, respectively (Fig. S16a2, b2, and c2). TEM images showed that the average diameters of C-CDs, H-CDs, and L-CDs were 2.14 nm, 3.25 nm, and 3.22 nm, respectively (Fig. S16a3, b3, and c3). The average diameters of H-CDs and L-CDs were similar to the CDs (3.5 nm) from BMPs. Fluorescence spectroscopy analysis revealed that the fluorescence emission peaks of these three types of CDs were close to that of the CDs in BMPs (Fig. S16a4, b4, and c4). However, the fluorescence intensity of H-CDs and L-CDs was 23 times and 19 times higher than that of C-CDs, respectively. Therefore, it is possible that the CDs in BMPs were mainly generated from hemicellulose and lignin in the sawdust.

Next, the effect of CDs and CSs in BMPs on the water evaporation rate was investigated. After reducing the lignin content in the sawdust from 28.3 % to 3 % by NaClO2 treatment according to a previously reported method [62,63], this treated sawdust was used to react with sulfuric acid, and the obtained product was named CDs-SD. As shown in Fig. S17a, it was observed that compared to BMPs, the formation of nanospheres on the surface of CDs-SD is significantly reduced. Then, CDs-SD was mixed with BC (the weight ratio is 7:3) to obtain CDs-SD-BC film. The color of CDs-SD-BC film (Fig. S17b) was less dark compared to BMPs-BC (Fig. S18), and its light absorption capacity also decreased from 94.7 % to 85 % when compared to BMPs-BC (Fig. S17c). Water evaporation tests showed that the water evaporation rate of CDs-SD-BC evaporator was 2.34 kg m−2 h−1 (Fig. S17d), which was significantly lower than that of BMPs-BC (3.53 kg m−2 h−1). To further investigate the impact of CSs on the rate of water evaporation, the CSs in BMPs were destroyed by ultrasonication, and the obtained product was named US-BMPs. As shown in Fig. S19a, most of the CSs were broken after this treatment. The US-BMPs-BC film for evaporation was prepared by mixing BC with US-BMPs at a weight ratio of 7: 3. The color of the US-BMPs-BC film was black (Fig. S19b) and similar to BMPs-BC film. However, the light absorption rate (90.4 %) and evaporation rate (3.03 kg m−2 h−1) both were decreased compared to these of BMPs-BC film (Fig. S19c, d). Notably, the light absorption spectrum of the US-BMPs-BC film overlaps with that of the BMPs-BC film at wavelengths below 700 nm, but significantly decreases beyond 700 nm. This change is mainly due to the detachment of the CDs from the BMPs surface during ultrasonication crushing of CNSs, resulting in a decrease in light absorption. Furthermore, after removing the content of hemicellulose and lignin in sawdust through Soxhlet extraction and NaClO2 treatment, the treated sawdust was used to react with sulfuric acid, and the obtained product was named NLH-SD (NLH: no lignin and hemicellulose). As shown in the SEM image (Fig. S20a), the NLH-SD had very small amount of nanospheres. TEM image showed the content of the CDs was also decreased in NLH-SD (Fig. S20b). Then, a film of NLH-SD-BC was prepared by mixing NLH-SD and BC at a ratio of 7 to 3 following the same procedure for BMPs-BC film. However, compared to the black BMPs-BC film, the color of NLH-SD-BC film became brown (Fig. S20c), and its light absorption capacity decreased from 94.7 % to 74.5 % (Fig. S20d), and its evaporation rate was also decreased to only 1.71 kg m−2 h−1 (Fig. S20e). Thus, the CDs and CSs in BMPs both contribute to effective light absorption photothermal conversion.

The photothermal conversion ability is an important parameter for evaluating evaporators. The light absorption rate of BMPs-BC film measured using UV–Visible-NIR absorption spectroscopy was reached 94.7 % (Fig. 4a). The photothermal conversion ability of BMPs-BC film was evaluated by measuring the equilibrium surface temperature using an infrared camera (Fig. 5a). The results showed that the equilibrium surface temperature of BMPs-BC film reached 80.4 °C (Fig. 5b), which was much higher than the temperature at the evaporation interface during water evaporation (45.3 °C) (Fig. 4d), indicating its efficient conversion of sunlight into heat for water evaporation. Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) test showed that the optimal evaporation temperature was 91.2 °C for BMPs-BC (Fig. 5c). This temperature was lower than that of pure water (100 °C).

The enthalpy of water evaporation plays a crucial role in determining the evaporation rate, as it reflects the energy required for water molecules to transition from the liquid to vapor phase. The evaporation enthalpy of water is influenced by the state of water and the evaporation process. The evaporation enthalpy of water in pure water and BMPs-BC at 45 °C (the same temperature as the operating temperature of the evaporator) was measured using DSC. As shown in Fig. 5d, the evaporation enthalpy of pure water was approximately 2409.6 J g−1, close to the standard value of 2444 J g−1. In contrast, the evaporation enthalpy of water in BMPs-BC was only 1003.4 J g−1 (Fig. 5d), which is close to the equivalent vaporization enthalpy (1043 J g−1) calculated by the equation (Eq. (4)) [37].

Based on the above results, the BMPs-BC can effectively reduce the evaporation enthalpy of the internal water, thereby increasing the water evaporation rate. Furthermore, we used DSC testing to verify the water state within the BMPs-BC, as different states of water exhibit unique freezing-melting behaviors, for instance, strongly bound water exhibits no signal, the peak near 0 °C corresponds to intermediate water, and the broad peak represents free water [64,65]. As shown in Fig. 5e, compared to pure water, BMPs-BC exhibited a peak at 0 °C, indicating the formation of intermediate water. To further characterize the water state in BMPs-BC, we conducted Raman spectroscopy to analyze the interaction between water in BMPs and BC. Water in the material can be categorized as free water (FW), bound water (BW), and intermediate water (IW). Since IW interacts weakly with surrounding water molecules, its evaporation rate is faster than that of FW. As shown in Figs. 5f and S21, the IW/FW ratio of BMPs increased from 0.52 to 0.66 compared to sawdust, indicating an increase in IW due to sulfuric acid treatment, primarily resulting from the generation of hydrophilic SO₃H groups [66]. The IW/FW ratio of BC was even as high as 1.16 (Fig. 5g), Consequently, the IW/FW ratio of BMPs-BC further increased to 0.88 compared to BMPs (Fig. 5h). The presence of a large amount of intermediate water in BMPs-BC effectively reduces the enthalpy of evaporation. Therefore, the BMPs-BC evaporator can achieve an evaporation rate of up to 3.52 kg m−2 h−1 and a solar energy-to-vapor generation efficiency of 91.1 % under one sun irradiation (Eq. (3)).

A comprehensive mechanism for the transition of water from liquid to vapor in the BMPs-BC evaporators has been suggested. Water can be categorized into three distinct types in a BMPs-BC system: free water (FW), intermediate water (IW), and bound water (BW) [67,68]. As shown in Fig. 6a, water molecules in free water are primarily stabilized by hydrogen bonds and form clusters. Therefore, traditional water evaporation processes depend significantly on external energy to break these hydrogen bonds [69]. The water that interacts weakly with the evaporator is referred to as “FW”. In contrast, as depicted in Fig. 6b, an evaporator is rich in hydrophilic groups that strongly interact with water molecules. Those water molecules that are drawn to the evaporator's surface through strong non-covalent interactions and establish stable hydrogen bonds are classified as “BW”. Additionally, water that is neither completely bound nor entirely free is identified as “IW” (Fig. 6c). This type of water forms primarily arises from the influence of BW, which forms stable hydrogen bonds with the evaporator, thereby impacting the adjacent water molecules. These adjacent water molecules interact with fewer than four other water molecules, preventing the formation of stable molecular clusters like those found in FW, thereby creating an IW layer adjacent to BW. Moreover, IW requires less energy to evaporate compared to FW or BW due to its weaker hydrogen bonding.

The BC is rich in hydroxyl functional groups, which allow it a strong attraction to water, enabling it to rapidly transport water from below the evaporator to the surface, thus effectively replenishing any water has been evaporated. Additionally, because the BC was coated on the surface of the BMPs, the water that was transported creates an ultrathin layer. This phenomenon not only reduces heat loss that usually occurs when heating large amounts of water but also lowers the proportion of FW, which in turn decreases the enthalpy of evaporation (Fig. 6d). As shown in Fig. 6e, the hydrophilic network structure of the BC interacts well with water molecules, leading to the generation of a considerable amount of IW. Furthermore, the surface of BMPs is enhanced with sulfonic acid groups that also interact with water molecules, further contributing to the formation of IW (Fig. 6f). Lastly, the abundant nanospheres and CDs presented on BMPs absorb sunlight, creating localized high-temperature regions that efficiently convert surrounding liquid water into vapor (Fig. 6g).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real evaporation effects of BMPs-BC evaporator
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the practical application potential of the BMPs-BC evaporators, a test was conducted on the roof top of a building at the University of the Chinese Academy of Sciences in Beijing, China. The water harvesting device was homemade with acrylic sheets and its dimensions were shown in Fig. S22. The evaporator arrays was made up of forty pieces of BMPs-BC films with a diameter of 1.5 cm and placed inside of the harvesting device (Fig. 7a1). After operating under sunlight for 30 min, as shown in Fig. 7a2, water vapor has already condensed into lots of water droplets upon contact with the acrylic sheet and were then dropped to the bottom of the device. During the tests, the maximum solar irradiation was 944 W m−2 at 12:06 (Fig. 7b), and the peak temperature was 29.6 °C at 16:10 (Fig. 7c). The maximum water production rate during the tests was 2.86 kg m−2 h−1, and the cumulative water obtained from 9:00 to 18:00 (9 h) was 15.9 kg m−2 (Fig. 7d). By measuring the concentrations of four major ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+) in seawater and collected freshwater, it can be observed that the ion concentrations decreased by 3 to 4 orders of magnitude, complying the drinking water standards set by the World Health Organization (WHO) (Fig. 7e) [70–72]. This result show that the BMPs-BC evaporator have great potential for practical solar-driven evaporation applications.

In addition, to evaluate the long-term stability of the evaporation device, we continuously measured the evaporation rates of the device on sunny days during March, April, and May (Fig. S23-S31). These results are summarized in Fig. 7f, demonstrate a clear correlation between the maximum evaporation rate and both environmental temperature and solar irradiation. Specifically, after three months of outdoor sunlight exposure, the BMPs-BC evaporator arrays demonstrated a stable evaporation rate exceeding 2 kg m−2 h−1 in seawater containing approximately 3.2 wt% NaCl. Importantly, no salt crystallization was observed during this long period. Throughout the desalination process, the ambient operating temperature ranged from 15 to 29 °C, and solar flux was maintained between 0.84 kW m−1 and 1.01 kW m−1 (Fig. 7f). At the lower ambient temperature of 15 °C, the maximum recorded evaporation rate was 2.17 kg m−2 h−1, while at the higher temperature of 29 °C, the rate increased to 2.86 kg m−2 h−1. Notably, during the three-month testing period, no obvious structural damage was observed in the BMPs-BC evaporator arrays (Fig. S32). Furthermore, the light absorption rate remained at 93.9 % and the indoor evaporation rate maintained at 3.50 kg m−2 h−1 under one sun irradiation on a BMPs-BC film after using in the outdoor testing for three months (Fig. S33, 34). Both results were very close to those of the BMPs-BC film measured before evaporation testing. Thus, the CDs can maintain their structural stability without obvious decomposition during evaporator operation. This suggests that the excellent stability and efficient photothermal conversion performance of the BMPs-BC evaporator arrays over months in real outdoor environment. The BMPs-BC evaporators' stable performance can be attributed to two key factors: (i) The excellent hydrophilicity of bacterial cellulose allows for efficient transport of high-concentration saltwater from the evaporator surface to the water reservoir. This prevents the accumulation of salt crystals, which could otherwise hinder the evaporation rate. (ii) Due to the inherent antibacterial properties of CDs [73–76], the incorporation of carbon dots provides antibacterial properties, effectively protecting the evaporator from bacterial growth and associated degradation, further enhancing the device's durability and performance. These combined features highlight the potential of the BMPs-BC evaporators for sustainable, high-efficiency seawater desalination in practical, long-term applications.

This study developed a simple and low-cost method for preparing sustainable photothermal conversion materials that exhibit excellent performance in solar-driven water evaporation. Using only sulfuric acid treatment, we successfully converted waste sawdust into bitter melon-like particles that composed of CDs, CSs, and cellulose. Under sulfuric acid treatment, lignin in the sawdust polymerizes to form nanospheres, while hemicellulose and some lignin undergo hydrolysis. The hydrolyzed fragments subsequently polymerize and carbonize into CDs. Compared to traditional hydrothermal and microwave methods, this approach avoids high-pressure and high-temperature conditions, thereby simplifying the reaction processes. Using sawdust as a raw material not only effectively addresses waste disposal issues but also significantly reduces material costs. The evaporators made from BMPs-BC demonstrated outstanding water evaporation performance, achieving a water evaporation rate of 3.53 kg m−2 h−1 under one sun irradiation at an ambient temperature of 20 °C. The BMPs-BC evaporators have an absorbance of 94.6 % across the entire solar spectral range. Additionally, the BMPs-BC evaporators showed excellent stability and salt resistance, maintaining a consistent water evaporation rate with no salt deposition on the surface during 24 h of continuous operation. In outdoor measurements, the maximum water production rate of the BMPs-BC evaporator arrays was 2.86 kg m−2 h−1, with a cumulative water yield of 15.9 kg m−2 over 9 h. Continuous outdoor measurements from March to May further confirmed the stability of the BMPs-BC evaporators. This research provides new possibilities for the sustainable utilization of waste biomaterials, particularly for applications in efficient and cost-effective photothermal materials for solar-driven water production.

Chunyu Ji: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis. Ting Wang: Visualization, Validation, Formal analysis. Yifeng Wang: Investigation. Qian Ding: Validation, Investigation. Zhuoning Xu: Methodology. Zhili Peng: Conceptualization. Han Yang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
